canon.gloss: The amount a garment is nipped in at the midsection so it flares from a tight waist.
canon.principle: The eye reads shape by contrast. Take the waist in hard and the chest and skirt read larger by comparison — the X-line, the inverted triangle. The 'Superman build' is not added mass; it is removed waist.
canon.quickRead: A coat fits roughly straight up and down.
canon.closerRead: The silhouette is sculpted — suppression at the waist, deliberate flare below — building the V by subtraction.

echo.note: The same instinct as ma in architecture — the form is defined by what is removed, not what is added.

canon.gloss: A small armscye set high under the arm, with a lean sleeve.
canon.principle: A high armhole lets the arm lift without dragging the whole coat body with it — so the torso stays clean and still. Counterintuitively, smaller armhole = more mobility.
canon.closerRead: High and closed: the arm moves, the body stays still. The negative space at the side seam is a tell of how the armhole was cut.

echo.note: The arm-gap is interval — the deliberate void that lets the form breathe.

canon.gloss: A cut (London/military lineage, Scholte at Anderson & Sheppard) with a little deliberate fullness over the chest and shoulder blades and a clean, suppressed waist.
canon.principle: A whisper of extra cloth over the chest creates a soft vertical ripple — 'drape' — that reads as depth and power, then the waist is cut close so the contrast lands. It is the opposite of shrink-wrap slim.
canon.closerRead: Fullness where volume reads as depth, suppression where the waist narrows — cloth deployed in deliberate contrast.

echo.note: Like poché in a plan — mass and void deployed on purpose, not by accident.

canon.gloss: An undarted, straight-hanging jacket with natural shoulders and no waist shaping (the American 'sack').
canon.principle: No darts, no suppression: the cloth falls straight from the shoulder. Comfortable, democratic, and a silhouette with its own clear logic.
canon.closerRead: Knowing it by name clarifies what it does: no darts, no suppression, the cloth falls straight. A useful reference point.

echo.note: The tailoring equivalent of letting the material speak — absence of artifice, for better or worse.

canon.gloss: A sleevehead set with a small raised roll of wadding so the shoulder line lifts at the seam.
canon.principle: A built, roped shoulder squares and widens the top of the X; its opposite, the Neapolitan spalla camicia (shirt-shoulder), is soft and sloped. Roped = structure and authority; soft = ease.
canon.closerRead: A clean roped shoulder is structure made visible — the upper bar of the X, chosen deliberately.

echo.note: Tectonic, in Frampton's sense — the expressed joint, structure made visible.

canon.gloss: The height of the seam where the collar meets the lapel.
canon.principle: A higher gorge pulls the eye up and lengthens the line; a low gorge widens and shortens. On a near-lapel-less coat, the collar break does the same job. Small seam, large effect on proportion.
canon.closerRead: Gorge height is a proportion line — a single horizontal that affects every proportion below it, like a datum in a plan.

echo.note: A datum: the reference line that organises everything measured against it.

canon.gloss: The lower body of a coat flaring outward from the waist toward the hem.
canon.principle: Below the suppressed waist, the skirt opens into an A. Combined with hard waist suppression it completes the hourglass-over-trousers read and lets the coat clear the thighs when seated — function and form in the same cut.
canon.closerRead: Below the suppressed waist, the skirt opens into an A — function (it clears the thighs when seated) and form (it completes the hourglass read) in the same cut.

echo.note: The skirt is threshold — a mediating space between form and movement.

canon.gloss: A collar that stands up around the neck with no lapel — funnel (tall, sweeping), mandarin/Nehru (short band), Stehkragen (the loden tradition).
canon.principle: Removing the lapel removes the busiest element on a coat. What remains is line. A stand collar frames the head like a plinth frames a column.
canon.quickRead: A coat needs a lapel to look finished.
canon.closerRead: Removing the lapel removes the busiest element. What remains is line. A stand collar frames the head like a plinth frames a column.

echo.note: Subtraction as honesty — say only what the structure needs.

canon.gloss: 'Raw concrete' — concrete left as cast, formwork grain and all. The root of the word Brutalism.
canon.principle: Brutalism is not 'brutal'; it is brut, raw. The material is shown doing its job with no facing. Honesty as an aesthetic position — the building admits what it is made of.
canon.closerRead: The board-marks read as a record of how the thing was made — the formwork's fingerprint. Material displayed doing its job.

echo.note: Truth to materials — the same value as celebrating patina rather than hiding it.

canon.gloss: Frampton's pair (after Semper): tectonic = the lightweight frame, the expressed joint; stereotomic = the heavy mass, the carved or stacked earthwork.
canon.principle: Every building leans one way: it either expresses its frame and connections (tectonic — a timber joint, a steel node) or reads as carved mass (stereotomic — a stone plinth, a poured wall). Naming which one clarifies what the architect chose to celebrate.
canon.closerRead: Every building leans one way: it either expresses its frame and connections, or reads as carved mass. Naming which one clarifies what the architect chose to celebrate.

echo.note: Tectonic in clothing is the roped shoulder; stereotomic is the heavy melton mass of a greatcoat.

canon.gloss: In a plan drawing, the solid mass (walls, structure) shown filled in black; the rooms are the white voids it shapes.
canon.principle: Poché flips your attention: the wall is not leftover, it is a sculpted solid, and the room is the void it carves. Learn to read the black and you see space as a positive thing being shaped, not empty leftover.
canon.quickRead: A floor plan shows where the rooms are.
canon.closerRead: Read the solid as the figure, the room as the ground — sometimes inverting which is which. The wall is sculpted, not leftover.

echo.note: Mass and void deployed on purpose — the same logic as drape and suppression.

canon.gloss: A run of rooms whose doorways are aligned, so you see straight through a sequence of spaces in one vista.
canon.principle: Align the openings and you manufacture depth and ceremony — the eye travels through five rooms at once. It is choreography in plan: the building tells you where to look and walk.
canon.closerRead: Align the openings and depth becomes ceremony — the eye travels through five rooms at once. Choreography in plan.

echo.note: A rhythm of openings and walls — interval again, at building scale.

canon.gloss: The Japanese concept of negative space, interval, the charged emptiness between things — in time as well as space.
canon.principle: Ma is the pause that gives the notes meaning: the gap, the breath, the interval. A room, a garden, a sentence, a silhouette — all are composed by their empty parts as much as their full ones.
canon.closerRead: Compose with the void deliberately — the gap is the design, not the absence of one. The pause that gives the notes meaning.

echo.note: The arm-gap on a coat is ma — deliberate negative space that defines the form.

canon.gloss: Le Corbusier's ground-level columns that lift a building's mass off the earth, leaving the ground floor open.
canon.principle: Raise the mass on slim columns and you do two things: free the ground to flow underneath, and make a heavy volume appear to float. Weight and lightness staged against each other.
canon.closerRead: Raise the mass on slim columns: free the ground to flow underneath, make a heavy volume appear to float. Weight and lightness staged against each other.

echo.note: Lifting the mass to free movement underneath — the building's high armhole.

canon.gloss: Japanese minimalism crossed with Scandinavian warmth and function: low, natural, quiet, but never cold.
canon.principle: Two minimalisms with opposite temperatures. Japan brings restraint, asymmetry, and reverence for material; Scandinavia brings warmth, light wood, and hygge comfort. The blend avoids the chill of pure minimalism.
canon.quickRead: A quiet, liveable blend of Japanese restraint and Scandinavian warmth.
canon.closerRead: Two minimalisms with opposite temperatures — Japanese restraint and asymmetry, Nordic warmth and light. A room might draw more from one half or the other.

echo.note: Its soul is wabi-sabi; its comfort is hygge.

canon.gloss: The Japanese aesthetic of beauty in imperfection, impermanence, and incompleteness.
canon.principle: Perfection is sterile and dead; the crack, the wear, the asymmetry are where life and time show. A repaired bowl (kintsugi) is more beautiful for the repair. Beauty that admits it will pass.
canon.closerRead: True wabi-sabi is earned and restrained — the crack that shows use, the repair that shows care, distinct from manufactured 'distressing.'

echo.note: Patina is wabi-sabi made literal — value accruing through use.

canon.gloss: Understated, unobtrusive beauty — refined, restrained, revealing more the longer you look.
canon.principle: Shibui is the opposite of flashy. It is quiet on first glance and rewards sustained attention: simple surface, complex depth. Beauty that does not announce itself.
canon.closerRead: Shibui rewards sustained attention: a quiet surface with complex depth. The object that reveals more the longer you look.

echo.note: Shibui in dress is the grey wool coat — restraint with a single charged detail.

canon.gloss: Design that connects occupants to nature — light, growth, natural material, view, water, organic form.
canon.principle: Humans evolved outdoors; spaces that reintroduce nature measurably lower stress and lift cognition. Not a potted plant in the corner — daylight, prospect, natural materials, and organic geometry working together.
canon.closerRead: The whole nature-connection system working together: daylight strategy, natural materials, sightline to green, organic geometry.

echo.note: Pairs naturally with japandi — both want the natural world brought quietly indoors.

canon.gloss: The surface change a material earns through age and use — the sheen on worn brass, leather, oiled wood.
canon.principle: Patina is value that cannot be bought new, only accrued. It marks an object as honest (it shows its life) and as quality (cheap material degrades; good material patinas). The opposite of the disposable.
canon.closerRead: Patina marks material as honest — it shows its life — and as quality: good material patinas where cheap material degrades.

echo.note: The material telling the truth about its life — same value as béton brut.

canon.gloss: The modernist principle that a material should look like itself — wood as wood, concrete as concrete — never faked or veneered to imitate another.
canon.principle: Honesty as a design ethic: don't print wood-grain on plastic, don't paint steel to look like stone. Let each material show its real nature and do its real job. The discipline behind much of good minimalism.
canon.quickRead: Materials chosen for how they look.
canon.closerRead: The discipline of material honesty: don't fake what something is made of, let each material do its real job. The principle behind much of good minimalism.

echo.note: Subtraction as honesty — the lapel-less coat saying only what it needs to.
